facts_disclosed: Maia's Year 7 camp Hanmer Springs Feb 14

turns.content: okay so i have a situation coming up that i need to mentally prepare for and i'm not sure whether i'm overthinking it or underthinking it
Hit me with it — sometimes talking it through helps you figure out which one it is. What's the situation?

my daughter has her school camp starting this friday, february 14, up at hanmer springs. four nights. year 7 camp, big deal for the kids. and both me and her dad have put our names down as parent helpers which means we're going to be spending four days together in a way we haven't done since we separated

Ah, okay. So it's not the camp itself that's the issue — it's the co-parenting dynamic in close quarters for an extended stretch. How long has it been since you and her dad were around each other for more than a handoff or a school event?

we do pickups and dropoffs fine, we can do a school concert together, that kind of thing. but four days sleeping in the same camp, eating meals together, being parent helpers on the same activities? that's a whole different level eh. we haven't spent that much continuous time together since the split. and we're both fine, we co-parent really well, but there's a difference between doing half an hour in a school carpark and doing four days

You're not overthinking it — that's a genuine shift from brief, structured interactions to an extended, unstructured stay. Even when the co-parenting relationship is solid, four days of shared space brings up dynamics that a 15-minute handoff never touches.

A few things to think about:

1. **For your daughter** — how does she feel about both of you being there? Is she excited, nervous, or has she not thought about it much?
2. **Boundaries** — have you and her dad talked about how you'll handle it? Like, are you going to function as a unit or more independently?
3. **Other parents** — how much do the other parents know about your situation? That social layer can add its own pressure.

Maia is stoked actually, she thinks it's hilarious that both of us are coming. she's at that age where she finds her parents slightly embarrassing but also secretly loves the attention eh. Wiremu and I haven't really talked about how we'll play it, that's probably the thing i need to sort out before friday. and the other parents know we're separated, most of them know us both, it's christchurch not exactly a huge city

Maia being relaxed about it is a really good sign — kids pick up on tension fast, so if she's genuinely happy about it, that says a lot about how you and Wiremu have handled things.

For the conversation with Wiremu before Friday, I'd suggest keeping it simple and practical:

- **Activities**: Are you both doing the same ones, or splitting up so Maia gets variety? (Also takes pressure off you being side by side all day)
- **Meals and downtime**: Sit together or not? Follow Maia's lead on that
- **The other parents**: Are you comfortable being seen together as a team, or would you prefer some space? Either is fine
- **An exit plan**: If things feel weird, having a signal or agreement that one of you takes a walk is healthy, not a failure

Do you think Wiremu would be open to that kind of chat, or would he find it excessive?

he'd be fine with it, Wiremu is pretty easygoing. actually that's one of the things that makes the co-parenting work, he doesn't get wound up about stuff. i'm probably the one who needs to chill out about it. i think the splitting activities idea is smart because then Maia gets us both without it being this awkward family unit performance eh

That's a good instinct. Splitting activities means Maia gets the best of both of you, the other kids see you both as individual helpers rather than a "couple," and you and Wiremu get breathing room. You naturally come together for meals and evenings without it being forced.

And I'd say — give yourself permission to actually enjoy it. Hanmer Springs is beautiful, your daughter is at a fun age for camp, and you clearly have a co-parenting relationship that works. It doesn't have to be stressful. It might actually be nice.

you're right and i think part of my anxiety is that it might be TOO nice and then i'll start questioning everything. like what if we get along so well that Maia starts hoping we'll get back together? she's smart but she's still 11 and kids read into things

That's a very perceptive concern. Kids absolutely do read into things, and an 11-year-old watching her parents laugh and cooperate for four days could easily build a story in her head.

A couple of thoughts:

- Getting along well at camp doesn't undo the reality of your separation. You can be warm, cooperative, and even have fun together without it meaning anything has changed. That's actually the healthiest possible modelling for Maia.
- If you're worried about her reading into it, you could have a low-key chat with her before camp: "Dad and I are both coming and we're going to have a great time, but nothing's different with us, okay? We're just both here for you."
- Kids at 11 are also more capable of nuance than we sometimes give them credit for. She might surprise you.

Have you had conversations with her before about this kind of thing, or is it more unspoken?

we've talked about it a bit. when we first split she asked a lot of questions but she's settled now. she understands that mum and dad are better as friends who parent together than as a couple. i just don't want to rock that by giving her confusing signals. but you're right, i think a quick chat before we leave is a good idea. just name it so it's not the elephant in the room

Exactly — naming it takes the power out of it. A quick, casual mention rather than a big sit-down conversation. Something like "this is going to be fun, both of us being there" with the implicit message that it's normal, not special.

And the same goes for you and Wiremu. If you can both be relaxed and natural, Maia will take her cue from that. Kids read anxiety more than they read the actual situation.

okay this has been really helpful. i'm going to text Wiremu tonight and suggest we split the activity groups and just play it relaxed. and i'll have the low-key chat with Maia tomorrow night. hanmer springs is gorgeous so honestly worst case scenario i'm spending four days in hot pools and bush walks with my girl and that's not exactly a hardship eh

That's the spirit. Hot pools, bush walks, your daughter having a blast at camp — there are worse ways to spend four days. And the fact that you're thinking this carefully about how everyone feels, including Wiremu, tells me you'll navigate it well.

Text Wiremu, chat with Maia, pack your togs for the hot pools, and go have fun. You've got this.
togs are already packed, first thing i sorted. priorities eh. thanks for the kōrero, it's helped me get out of my own head about it. kia kaha
Ha, togs first — that's the right call. Have an amazing time at camp. Kia kaha to you too.
